title: Portland City Council Work Session  FY Expenditure Forecast & Spring TAO 03/05/26

chapters.summary: Council to hold afternoon work session on the Spring TAO on Thursday, May 5th. For the record, Jonas Beery, the city's chief financial officer. Everybody's fed, hydrated, happy, ready to go.
chapters.headline: Council President orders afternoon work session on Spring TAO on Thursday, May 5

chapters.summary: City Budget Office Director Levine will walk through the numbers, including an update on the remaining fiscal '25-'26 deficit and some technical details. And then I'll jump back in and review some recommendations and potential solutions to fill that deficit. There actually is a little bit of good news, at least in context.
chapters.headline: Council President, we have a budget deficit of $18 million

chapters.summary: Council identified an additional gap for this fiscal year, which was communicated in an email from City Administrator Lee to City Council on January 7th, 2026. A series of potential solutions were identified, many of which required additional due diligence. And so we've now completed most of that due diligence and are coming back with some suggestions.
chapters.headline: Council previously discussed fall technical adjustment ordinance in November 2025

chapters.summary: The goals are to update you on the status, to discuss the options and to support council's discussion to inform what will ultimately be in the actual ordinance. Ordinance for council adoption and consideration. Some of the numbers that we're going to talk about are subject to change based upon input we get from Council.
chapters.headline: The goals of today's work session are to update you on budget issues

chapters.summary: On the revenue side, we're tracking about $3.5 million below the December forecast for the current year, primarily driven by lower ULF revenues. We'll be updating the March forecast in a few weeks, and that will set the baseline for the mayor's proposed closed.
chapters.headline: Ruth Levine: We're tracking about $3.5 million below December forecast

chapters.summary: Councilor Green: How much exactly is compensation set aside contributing to this projected deficit? Jonas: In terms of what we need to distribute now in the spring, do we have a number for that?
chapters.headline: Councilor Green: How much compensation set aside contributes to projected deficit
chapters.gist: Councilor Green on Compensation Set Aside and the Budget

chapters.summary: The city has a $18 million gap to fill. Here are the options that we think should be in the conversation to solve for that. There's trade-offs to any option, but we've aimed to prioritize solutions that avoid staffing or service impacts. These are all subject to council.
chapters.headline: We wanted to walk through options to solve for that $18 million gap
chapters.gist: Options for Filling the General Fund Gap

chapters.summary: $2.2 million reflects aggregate total among all council offices, excluding council operations. Defer to your direction about how to direct underspending dollars. Third item is one we talked about in the fall. Citywide obligation reserve Reserve Fund. This might be a good natural pause 'cause that's a lot to digest.
chapters.headline: There have been suggestions to return surplus Housing Investment Fund dollars to general fund

chapters.summary: $3.39 million in underspending. Biggest buckets are $500,000 roughly for graffiti reduction. Who holds the POIC contract that we're talking about? It's now under Ceasefire. Any other questions, colleagues?
chapters.headline: Anthony Anthony: I have 3 questions regarding underspending in public safety
chapters.gist: Portland Budget Office: $3.39 Million in underspending

chapters.summary: In addition to rebalancing the general fund in the spring TAO, there will be a number of other adjustments. The other thing we wanted to highlight, we talked to you about this back in the fall, is the health fund. The situation is a little bit worse than we anticipated.
chapters.headline: In addition to rebalancing the general fund, there will be other adjustments

chapters.summary: Councilor Kunal: I have at least 6 hours of requested items for the first Committee of the Whole meeting. Councilor Murillo: I appreciate all the folks running meetings and trying to schedule things. So I think we can pause there and turn it over for questions.
chapters.headline: Councilor Connell: I have at least 6 hours of requested items
chapters.gist: Committee of the Whole Meeting Calendar

chapters.summary: A proposal to use police asset forfeiture funds to cover $1.4 million of event overtime. The funds can't be used to supplant existing budget. So it has to be more opportunistic opportunistic when it's the asset forfeiture dollars.
chapters.headline: There's a proposal to use police asset forfeiture funds to cover event overtime
chapters.gist: PBB to Use Police Asset forfeiture Funds to Cover Overtime

chapters.summary: How do you plan to proactively incorporate these dollars in order to help address the deficit that the general fund is facing? Can we proactively do that so that there's— so that we can use more of that fund?
chapters.headline: How do you plan to incorporate these dollars to help address general fund deficit

chapters.summary: I had another question not regarding the special revenue fund. Regarding the technology replacement reserve for laptops. Will the use of any of the BTS tech reserves put any bureaus in a position to unable to meet their current technology replacement needs?
chapters.headline: Councilor Murillo: Regarding the technology replacement reserve for laptops

chapters.summary: Council President: I'm concerned that what we're doing by not looking at program changes is creating an even more difficult situation for ourselves next fiscal year. The implementation of those kinds of reductions is much more challenging it in a late-stage fiscal year, he says. Councilor: Help me understand how compensation set aside is being used here.
chapters.headline: Council President: I'm concerned that not looking at program changes is counterproductive

chapters.summary: Councilor Green: Are those reductions to areas that were previously approved in the fall too as encumbrance carryovers? Council President Connell: What I would ask for before we're asked to take a vote on this ordinance is a spreadsheet that shows the current state of play of those EMS contracts.
chapters.headline: Councilor Tao asks about 2 million EMS reductions that could be implemented

chapters.summary: Public safety overspending is comparable to previous years, but not apples to apples. Any amount carried over in a prior year needs to appear as funded in the adopted. This is why I've been asking the administrator for the big contracts going forward.
chapters.headline: Public safety overspending is comparable to previous years, but not apples to apples

chapters.summary: Councilor: Can you tell us more about the Harbor Reserve Fund? City anticipates some type of obligation related to harbor cleanup activities. Effectively draining this would require the future year replenishment. Councilor: There will need to be conversations about how much of that is actually real.
chapters.headline: Councilor: Can you tell us more about the Harbor Reserve Fund

chapters.summary: This morning I thought that when the pot of housing money that was easier to shift to the general fund was the quote short-term rental 2%, $8.6 million. I'm pretty sure it's the 0 to 60 subfund. I want to make sure that there aren't pieces of that that might have some firmer expectations around it.
chapters.headline: Councilor Novick: I was surprised to see short-term rental pot
chapters.gist: Short-term rental 0 to 60 subfund referred to

chapters.summary: President Dunphy: How did you come to $4 million as opposed to the full $6.6 million? Councilor: I think this is an an overestimation, definitely.
chapters.headline: President Dunphy: I think this is an overestimation, definitely
chapters.gist: President Dunphy on the Housing Investment Shortfall

chapters.summary: The Harbor Reserve combines multiple different different sort of colors of money into the reserve pot. A couple of these, whether it's the Housing Investment Fund or CORF, could be used as sort of balancers. Some of these are scalable in different, different ways.
chapters.headline: The Harbor Reserve combines multiple different colors of money into the reserve pot

chapters.summary: Councilor Kunal asks, which contracts are you talking about? I think I'm going to hold off on naming specific ones until the list comes out. There's contracts we have for people who think for us. That kind of contracting I think is, is worth looking at.
chapters.headline: Councilor Kunal says there are some contracts worth looking at
chapters.gist: Other Contracts on the City Budget

chapters.summary: Councillor Green: In the fall, I offered an amendment that would've required a real look at service reductions and given us time to actually enact them. Instead of an $18 million shortfall, we would have a $7 million shortfall. I worry that we could find ourselves in a similar situation next fall.
chapters.headline: Councillor Maria Green expresses frustration over proposed service reductions

chapters.summary: Councilor Connell: Is that OEO the place to do it if we want to be very explicit about what it is we're going to adopt in the '26-'27? Councilor Green: It's probably more prudent to allow those funds to fall to balance rather than deappropriating them in the current fiscal year.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green: I'm cautioning against carrying over as a practice

chapters.summary: Councilor Kernor: The projected savings associated with that had not fully manifested. He says the only function that's really been effectively fully implemented is communications. Kernor says there may be something to learn in terms of being more granular instead of a blunt instrument.
chapters.headline: Council President Kern says projected savings associated with the 10% reduction have not manifested

chapters.summary: I suddenly have a lot of concerns about our bond rating. Do you have any concerns about it at this point with these numbers? We'll have an updated revenue forecast in a few weeks before you vote on this. We're putting some of the latter at risk.
chapters.headline: Jon Jonas: I always have concern about our bond rating
chapters.gist: Sen. Jonas on Concerns About the Budget

chapters.summary: Council President: How do we fill the remaining 6.6? Documents. And then the third question I have is just around that timing. Is that something, a date we should be targeting, or are we moving to that first council date?
chapters.headline: Council President Ryan asks three questions regarding technical adjustment ordinance
chapters.gist: Third Question on the Technical Adjustment Ordinance

chapters.summary: Councilor Green: I do not support your suggested cuts, specifically for the council. Council President Smith: You just need to do a constraint across the board. Could you go back and just do a 1% constraint or a 0.05% constraint and see what that looks like?
chapters.headline: Councilor Green: I do not support your suggested cuts for council

chapters.summary: Council President: I am prepared to need to support everything on your first slide. Councillor Pirtle-Ginni: I'm not prepared to support a loan from our funds for the— out of the CORF. Councilor Kunal: I would be interested in seeing if there are things that the mayor will be cutting next year anyway.
chapters.headline: Council President asks colleagues if they would tolerate considering a $2.2 million cut

chapters.summary: Councilor Abalos: I'm uncomfortable with any cuts to any, uh, dipping further into any reserves. Councilor Green: The general reserve fund is for triggering in a rainy day. Council President Ryan: Of course I'm always up for cutting council office budgets.
chapters.headline: Council President says he does not support cutting council office budgets
chapters.gist: Council Member Questions Targeted Reduction in the Budget

chapters.summary: Thank you all so much for being here today. Um, did you get enough what you need at this moment? Yeah, we will treat this with as much urgency as it deserves. So colleagues, with that, we are adjourned.
chapters.headline: Council members: We will treat this with as much urgency as it deserves
